title: Deeply Fermented Chinese Pork Belly Char Siu Bao
description: A traditional Chinese dish featuring deeply fermented pork belly served in a steamed char siu bao, often consumed to cure hangovers. This recipe is suitable for a Buddhist temple-friendly setting.
instructions: In a large mixing bowl, combine pork belly, fermentation starter culture, sugar, and salt. Mix well until the sugar and salt are evenly distributed.
Cover the bowl with plastic wrap and let it sit at room temperature (75°F to 78°F) for 3 days, stirring the mixture every 12 hours.
After 3 days, transfer the mixture to the refrigerator and let it ferment for at least 7 days, stirring every 24 hours.
After 7 days, remove the pork belly from the refrigerator and let it sit at room temperature for 2 hours to allow the meat to come to room temperature.
Meanwhile, prepare the char siu bao dough according to the recipe.
Place a small amount of the fermented pork belly on the center of the dough, and fold the dough over the filling to form a bun shape.
Repeat the process until all the dough and pork belly are used up.
Steam the buns for 10-15 minutes, or until they are cooked through.
Let the buns rest for 30 minutes before serving.
Serve the buns with a side of vinaigrette dressing, such as soy sauce, vinegar, and sesame oil.
Garnish with scallions and sesame seeds, if desired.
